title: Writing a Dockerfile
description:
## Building Images With a Dockerfile

A **Dockerfile** is a plain-text recipe for an image. `docker build` reads it instruction-by-instruction, and **each instruction creates a new layer** stacked on the previous one.

### The instructions you'll meet first

| Instruction | What it does |
|---|---|
| `FROM <image>` | The **base** image to build on. Always the first instruction. |
| `WORKDIR <dir>` | Set the working directory for following instructions. |
| `COPY <src> <dst>` | Copy files from the build context into the image. |
| `RUN <cmd>` | Execute a command **at build time** (e.g. install packages) and commit the result as a layer. |
| `EXPOSE <port>` | Document which port the container listens on. |
| `CMD [...]` | The **default command** run when a container starts (overridable). |
| `ENTRYPOINT [...]` | The executable a container always runs; `CMD` becomes its default arguments. |

### A minimal example

```dockerfile
FROM alpine:latest
WORKDIR /app
COPY hello.txt .
CMD ["cat", "hello.txt"]
```

Build and run it:

```bash
docker build -t hello:1.0 .
docker run --rm hello:1.0
```

### Layer caching

Docker caches each layer. If an instruction and everything before it are unchanged, the cached layer is reused — which is why you put **rarely-changing** instructions (like dependency installs) **before** frequently-changing ones (like copying your source).

Mark this lesson complete, then build your own image in the next task.
